questions.specification:
According to the graph, which goal type shows the greatest overall increase in motivation score from week $1$ to week $6$?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9_hjei-B4j1AIiAupLYyf)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question compares outcome, performance, and process goal types and asks which best sustains/improves athlete motivation over time—directly assessing goal-setting types used to enhance motivation.

options.markscheme: Outcome goals decrease overall from week $1$ (6) to week $6$ (3).
Performance goals increase overall from week $1$ (6) to week $6$ (8), a net increase of $+2$.
Process goals increase overall from week $1$ (6) to week $6$ (8), a net increase of $+2$ (greatest increase; tied with performance goals).

options.content: None of the goal types increase from week $1$ to week $6$
options.markscheme: At least one goal type (performance and process) increases overall from week $1$ to week $6$.

options.markscheme: Incorrect: goal setting is a psychological strategy and does not primarily aim to reduce fatigue.

options.content: To enhance motivation and direct attention to tasks
options.markscheme: Correct: goal setting increases motivation and helps focus attention on relevant performance tasks.

options.content: To guarantee success in all competitions
options.markscheme: Incorrect: arousal regulation is a separate psychological skill; goal setting is primarily about motivation, focus, and direction.
Incorrect: this can be a benefit of goal setting, but the main purpose is to enhance motivation and direct attention toward specific tasks.

questions.specification:
The table shows goal statements from four athletes.

| Athlete | Goal statement |
| :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Improve push-off speed by activating the calves more during the sprint start |
| **B** | Beat my $800\text{ m}$ rival in the next track meet |
| **C** | Run $5\text{ km}$ in under 20 minutes by the end of the season |
| **D** | Keep eyes on the target and follow through in all free throw attempts |

The table classifies each goal statement by goal type.

| Athlete | Goal type |
| :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Process |
| **B** | Outcome |
| **C** | Performance |
| **D** | Process |

Which of the following statements is correct regarding these classifications?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to classify athletes’ goal statements into outcome, performance, and process goal types, which directly assesses understanding of goal setting and these goal categories.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Athletes A and D are process goals (technique/actions), B is an outcome goal (beating an opponent), and C is a performance goal (self-referenced time), so all classifications are correct.

Correct. Athletes A and D are process goals as they focus on specific technical actions. Athlete B is an outcome goal as it focuses on winning against an opponent. Athlete C is a performance goal as it focuses on achieving a personal standard (time).

options.content: Only A and C are correctly classified.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Athletes B and D are also correctly classified according to standard goal-setting theory.

options.content: Athlete B's goal should be performance, not outcome.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Athlete B's goal involves beating a rival, which is dependent on others' performances, making it an outcome goal. Performance goals are self-referenced standards.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.2 Imagery > C.5.2.1 Imagery mimics real experience using sensory modalities to improve performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.2.1 Imagery mimics real experience using sensory modalities to improve performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on imagery: internal vs external imagery, imagery under pressure, PETTLEP model for imagery in injury return, and combining imagery with physical practice—core content of the imagery psychological skill.

parts.content: Compare internal and external imagery, and explain when each is most appropriate.
parts.markscheme:
- Internal imagery: seeing through the athlete’s own perspective/eyes. <marks>A1</marks>
- External imagery: viewing oneself from a third-person perspective (e.g., as if watching a video). <marks>A1</marks>
- Internal imagery is most appropriate for enhancing kinesthetic awareness (e.g., feeling body positioning). <marks>A1</marks>
- External imagery is most appropriate for analyzing technique and overall movement patterns. <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 4 marks]

parts.content: Explain how imagery can enhance performance under high-pressure conditions.
parts.markscheme:
- Enhances preparedness by allowing athletes to mentally rehearse different competitive scenarios. <marks>A1</marks>
- Reduces anxiety through increased familiarity with stress-inducing situations. <marks>A1</marks>
- Boosts self-confidence and self-efficacy by simulating successful outcomes. <marks>A1</marks>
- Trains and maintains attentional control/focus on relevant cues under pressure. <marks>A1</marks>
- Improves emotional regulation by simulating and managing arousal responses. <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 5 marks]

parts.content: Discuss how the PETTLEP model can be used to structure imagery training for athletes returning from injury.
parts.markscheme:
- Physical: Athletes should adopt the same posture, clothing, and equipment as during performance to increase realism. <marks>A1</marks>
- Environment: Imagery should be conducted in the actual or a similar performance setting to increase contextual relevance. <marks>A1</marks>
- Task: The content of the imagery should match the specific technical and tactical demands of the sport-specific task. <marks>A1</marks>
- Timing: Imagery should be performed in real-time to reinforce the correct rhythm and duration of the movement. <marks>A1</marks>
- Learning: Imagery scripts should be updated to reflect the athlete's rehabilitation progress and skill development. <marks>A1</marks>
- Emotion: Athletes should incorporate the emotions they expect to feel (e.g., nerves, confidence) to simulate the competitive experience. <marks>A1</marks>
- Perspective: Imagery can be performed from an internal or external view depending on which is most vivid and effective for the athlete. <marks>A1</marks>

Note: Award [1] for each point discussed up to [6 marks] maximum.

[Total: 6 marks]

parts.content: Evaluate the effectiveness of combining imagery with physical practice in elite athletes.
parts.markscheme:
- Enhances skill retention particularly when physical training volume is limited by fatigue or injury. <marks>A1</marks>
- Reinforces self-confidence by allowing for mental repetitions of successful execution. <marks>A1</marks>
- Improves consistency by aligning mental routines with physical practice. <marks>A1</marks>
- Effectiveness may be limited if the athlete lacks the imagery skills of vividness or controllability. <marks>A1</marks>
- Optimal results are achieved when imagery is combined with expert guidance and regular feedback. <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 5 marks]

options.content: They perceive easy goals as more valuable than difficult ones
They experience a sense of deflation or reduced satisfaction after success
options.markscheme: The goal-setting paradox refers to the phenomenon where reaching a major, long-term goal results in a sense of emptiness or lack of direction rather than sustained happiness.

options.content: They only value external rewards, which limits their long-term motivation
Their motivation is unaffected by the achievement of their goals

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.2 The goal-setting paradox: elite athletes' dissatisfaction with achieving high goals (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks about the 'goal-setting paradox' and the options reference high goals improving performance but potentially lowering satisfaction, which matches this leaf topic on elite athletes’ dissatisfaction despite achieving high goals.

options.content: High goals result in higher performance but can result in lower satisfaction.
options.markscheme: Identify that high goals result in higher performance but can result in lower satisfaction. <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.content: Goal setting does not influence motivation.
Athletes are often more satisfied with harder goals.

questions.specification: According to the goal-setting paradox in sport psychology, why might achieving difficult goals not increase satisfaction?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.2 The goal-setting paradox: elite athletes' dissatisfaction with achieving high goals (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.2 The goal-setting paradox: elite athletes' dissatisfaction with achieving high goals (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks about the 'goal-setting paradox' and how it challenges traditional motivation theory—matching the HL leaf topic on elite athletes’ dissatisfaction despite achieving high goals (e.g., success not always increasing satisfaction).

options.content: It argues that only extrinsic rewards matter for performance.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: goal-setting theory and related critiques do not claim that performance depends only on extrinsic rewards.

options.content: It suggests that athletes tend to avoid difficult goals.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: goal-setting research typically emphasises that challenging (but attainable) goals can be adopted and can enhance performance.

options.content: It suggests that as goal difficulty increases, satisfaction may decrease because the standard for success is harder to meet and the perceived gap to the goal can remain large.
options.markscheme: Correct: the goal-setting paradox suggests that while difficult goals can raise performance, they may lower satisfaction because the criteria for success are harder to achieve and the perceived discrepancy between performance and the goal can remain greater.

options.content: It shows that setting goals permanently reduces intrinsic motivation.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: a permanent reduction in intrinsic motivation is an absolute claim and is not what the goal-setting paradox describes.

questions.specification: An elite runner achieves a new world record but feels less satisfied than after an easier race win. This best illustrates:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.2 The goal-setting paradox: elite athletes' dissatisfaction with achieving high goals (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.2 The goal-setting paradox: elite athletes' dissatisfaction with achieving high goals (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Scenario describes an elite athlete achieving an exceptionally high goal (world record) but feeling less satisfied than after an easier win—this is the classic ‘goal-setting paradox’ in elite performers.

options.markscheme: Correct: satisfaction can decrease after major achievements as the individual rapidly adapts to the new outcome/standard, so the world record brings less felt reward than expected.

options.content: The effect of extrinsic rewards undermining intrinsic motivation
options.markscheme: Plausible distractor: describes reduced intrinsic motivation when external rewards are salient, but the stem does not indicate external incentives or reward-contingencies.

Plausible distractor: satisfaction may drop if the athlete compares upward to others or new standards, but the key feature here is reduced satisfaction despite objective success, consistent with adaptation.

options.content: Overtraining causing reduced mood and enjoyment
options.markscheme: Plausible distractor: heavy training load can reduce mood/enjoyment, but the stem links the change specifically to achievement level rather than training-related fatigue symptoms.

Emphasizing process and performance goals alongside outcome goals
Focusing mainly on outcome goals (winning) to drive motivation
Setting too many goals at once to cover all areas of performance

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly references goal-setting theory and asks the effect of setting an overly easy goal on motivation, which aligns with goal setting as a motivational tool (appropriate difficulty/challenge).

options.content: The athlete feels challenged and motivated
options.markscheme: An easy goal is unlikely to increase challenge or effort; difficult goals tend to be more motivating.

options.content: The athlete may feel under-stimulated and less motivated
options.markscheme: Easy goals provide insufficient challenge, which can reduce effort and motivation.

options.content: The athlete is less likely to increase effort because the goal requires little challenge
options.markscheme: This reflects the mechanism behind reduced motivation (low challenge → low effort), but the question asks for the likely outcome; option B states this outcome more directly.

options.content: The athlete will show decreased persistence and commitment because the goal is not challenging
options.markscheme: Too-easy goals can reduce commitment/persistence, but option B is the most direct and generally expected outcome (reduced motivation).

questions.specification: The question evaluates knowledge and understanding of goal-setting strategies in sports psychology, including definitions, goal types, and the impact on motivation and confidence using the SMART principle and coaching interventions.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on defining goal setting, types of goals (eg outcome/performance), and how goals enhance motivation and confidence, including SMART goals and coaching strategies—core content of goal setting as a psychological skill.

parts.content: Define goal setting in the context of sports.
parts.markscheme:
- Goal setting is the process of identifying specific, measurable objectives to improve performance or motivation <marks>1</marks>
- Helps guide athlete focus and effort <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: State two types of goals athletes may set.
parts.markscheme:

- Process goals <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark per correct type. Performance goals is also an acceptable answer.</note>

parts.content: Outline three reasons why goal setting can enhance motivation.

parts.content: Explain how goal setting can influence an athlete’s confidence.

parts.content: Discuss how SMART goals are used in training and performance.

- Encourages focus and tracking progress <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Describe how a coach might help an athlete implement effective goal-setting strategies.

questions.specification: A cyclist achieves a challenging personal best but reports feeling less joy than after reaching a simpler goal. This reflects:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.2 The goal-setting paradox: elite athletes' dissatisfaction with achieving high goals (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.2 The goal-setting paradox: elite athletes' dissatisfaction with achieving high goals (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario describes achieving a very challenging goal (personal best) but experiencing less satisfaction/joy than after a simpler goal—this is the classic definition of the goal-setting paradox (elite athletes’ dissatisfaction after high-goal attainment).

options.markscheme: Attribution theory focuses on how individuals interpret events and how this relates to their thinking and behavior, particularly in explaining success and failure.
Self-determination theory concerns human motivation and personality, focusing on the degree to which an individual's behavior is self-motivated and self-determined.

The term “goal-setting paradox” is not a standard IB SEHS theory/term and is not the best fit for the reduced joy described compared with the intrinsic–extrinsic motivation explanation offered by self-determination theory.
Mental toughness theory relates to the ability of an individual to consistently perform toward the upper range of their talent and skill regardless of competitive circumstances.

questions.specification: Why do athletes often set performance goals?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why athletes set performance goals, with the correct option emphasizing enhanced focus and motivation—directly aligned with goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals.

options.content: To increase their focus and motivation during training and competition
options.markscheme: D

options.content: To compare their results with other athletes and win (outcome goals)
options.markscheme: D

options.content: To monitor progress and provide feedback on performance improvements
options.markscheme: D

options.content: To reduce performance pressure by keeping expectations low
options.markscheme: D

questions.specification: Which of the following is a potential benefit of clear goal setting?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about a benefit of clear goal setting (e.g., increased focus), which directly matches goal setting as a psychological skill used to enhance motivation via outcome/performance goals.

options.markscheme: Correct answer: A
Incorrect: clear goal setting can support (not reduce) motivation when goals are appropriate.

options.content: Increased anxiety due to uncertainty about expectations
options.markscheme: Incorrect: clear goals typically reduce uncertainty; anxiety may increase if goals are unrealistic, not simply due to goal clarity.

options.content: Lower adherence to training programmes because less feedback is needed
options.markscheme: Incorrect: clear goals often improve adherence and can be paired with feedback rather than replacing it.

questions.specification: Why might vague goals (e.g., "do better") be less effective than specific goals?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why vague goals are less effective than specific goals, focusing on clarity, direction, and measurability—core principles of goal setting in sport psychology (outcome/performance goals).

options.content: They provide too much flexibility, which can prevent consistent progress
They encourage focusing only on long-term outcomes rather than immediate actions
They lack clear direction and measurable outcomes
options.markscheme: Correct answer: D

questions.specification:
A coach works with an athlete during a 6-week pre-season phase. The athlete’s main long-term aim is to improve 5 km running performance from 22 min 00 s to 20 min 30 s by the end of week 6.

Table 1 shows weekly 5 km time-trial results and the athlete’s confidence rating (0–10).

| Week | 5 km time-trial (min:s) | Confidence rating (0–10) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 22:00 | 5 |
| 2 | 21:45 | 5 |
| 3 | 21:35 | 6 |
| 4 | 21:20 | 6 |
| 5 | 21:05 | 7 |
| 6 | 20:50 | 8 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on defining goal setting, types of goals (e.g., outcome/performance/process), links to motivation and confidence, SMART goals, and coach support—core content of goal setting as a psychological skill.

parts.content: Define goal setting in the context of sports.
parts.markscheme:
- Goal setting is the process of establishing objectives/targets for performance/training in sport. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Objectives are typically clear/specific and can be monitored/evaluated (for example measurable and time-bound). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as reference to goals providing direction/standards for performance.</note>

parts.content: State two types of goals athletes may set.
parts.markscheme:
- Outcome goals. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Performance goals. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as process goals, short-term goals, or long-term goals.</note>

parts.content: Outline three reasons why goal setting can enhance motivation.
parts.markscheme:
- Provides a clear focus and sense of purpose (clarifies what to work towards). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages persistence/commitment by providing small, achievable targets. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides a benchmark for progress and improvement, reinforcing training effort. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as improving planning/prioritization or time management.</note>

parts.content: Explain, using Table 1, how goal setting could influence the athlete’s confidence.
parts.markscheme:
- As performance improves across weeks (5 km time decreases from 22:00 to 20:50), successful attainment of targets can increase self-efficacy/self-belief. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Confidence rating increases from 5 (week 1) to 8 (week 6), consistent with confidence being reinforced by evidence of competence/progress. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Short-term/weekly goals (for example reducing time by small increments) can make improvement feel achievable, reducing fear of failure and promoting controllable focus. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reaching milestones (for example sub-21:00 by week 6 is approached at 20:50) can strengthen positive expectations for future performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as links to reduced anxiety/nerves or increased positive self-talk, provided they are linked to goal attainment/progress in Table 1.</note>

parts.content: Discuss how SMART goals could be used for this athlete’s training and performance across the 6 weeks.
parts.markscheme:
- Specific: define a clear target (for example reduce 5 km time) rather than a vague aim. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Measurable: use time-trial times/confidence ratings to track progress objectively each week. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Achievable/Realistic: set weekly targets aligned to current performance so goals are challenging but attainable. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Time-bound/Relevant: set deadlines within the 6-week period that align with the athlete’s long-term aim and training plan. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as accountability, sustained motivation, improved focus, and reduced distractions, provided they relate to applying the SMART framework to the scenario.</note>

parts.content: Explain how a coach might help this athlete implement effective goal-setting strategies.
parts.markscheme:
- Break the long-term goal into short-term process/performance goals for each week/session. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensure goals are individualized and at an appropriate level of challenge (adjust if too easy/too difficult). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Monitor progress regularly (for example review weekly time-trial data) and revise goals when needed. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provide feedback and reinforcement (for example acknowledge improvements/milestones) to maintain motivation and adherence. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as encouraging athlete autonomy in setting goals, using training logs, or linking imagery/visualization to goal attainment.</note>

questions.specification: According to SMART goal-setting principles, which type of goal is most effective in improving an athlete’s motivation?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which type of goal best improves athlete motivation; this directly aligns with goal setting as a psychological skill (e.g., using specific, measurable performance/outcome goals to enhance motivation).

options.markscheme: Vague goals lack specific direction and criteria for success, making it difficult for an athlete to track progress or stay motivated.
General goals are often too broad and do not provide the focus required to improve specific performance elements.
Specific and measurable goals (key components of the SMART principle) provide clear targets and allow for objective progress tracking, which are fundamental for maintaining high levels of motivation.
Challenges that are not structured or linked to performance objectives are less effective in providing sustained motivation compared to structured goal setting.

questions.specification: Which of the following is a key outcome of goal setting?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for a key outcome of goal setting; a central outcome is enhanced motivation, matching the topic on goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation via outcome and performance goals.

options.markscheme: Correct: goal setting can increase motivation by providing clear targets and feedback on progress.
Plausible outcome of goal setting, but not the best answer here because enhanced motivation is the key outcome targeted.
Plausible related psychological change, but goal setting is more directly associated with improving motivation and direction.
Goal setting can sometimes reduce anxiety via increased perceived control, but it is not the key outcome compared with motivation.

questions.specification: This question examines the psychological skill of imagery, its types, benefits, and practical application in sports performance and motor skill learning.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.2 Imagery > C.5.2.1 Imagery mimics real experience using sensory modalities to improve performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.2.1 Imagery mimics real experience using sensory modalities to improve performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on the psychological skill of imagery: definition, types, benefits, pre-performance use, structuring an imagery routine, and its role in performance and skill learning.

parts.content: Define imagery as a psychological skill.
parts.markscheme:
- Define imagery as the mental practice of visualizing movements or outcomes to enhance performance <marks>A1</marks>
- State that it involves creating or recreating experiences in the mind <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Identify two types of imagery used by athletes.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify visual imagery <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify kinesthetic imagery <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award marks for any two types, including: internal imagery or external imagery.</note>

parts.content: Outline three benefits of imagery in sports performance.
parts.markscheme:
- Enhance skill execution by mentally rehearsing movements <marks>A1</marks>
- Build confidence through visualization of success <marks>A1</marks>
- Reduce anxiety by promoting a sense of preparedness <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award marks for any three benefits, including: improved focus or reinforced strategy.</note>

parts.content: Explain how imagery can be used before a performance to prepare an athlete.
parts.markscheme:
- Rehearse skill execution mentally to prepare for the task <marks>A1</marks>
- Build confidence and reduce uncertainty through visualization <marks>A1</marks>
- Trigger pre-performance routines to establish focus <marks>A1</marks>
- Activate relevant neural pathways associated with the physical skill <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Discuss how imagery enhances motor skill learning.
parts.markscheme:
- Support the formation of motor programs by reinforcing neural circuits <marks>A1</marks>
- Reinforce technique through repetitive mental practice <marks>A1</marks>
- Complement physical training when fatigue or injury prevents physical practice <marks>A1</marks>
- Improve reaction time and anticipation by visualizing specific cues <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Describe how an athlete can structure an imagery routine effectively.
parts.markscheme:
- Set clear and realistic scenarios to mimic actual performance conditions <marks>A1</marks>
- Use all senses, including visual, auditory, and kinesthetic, to increase vividness <marks>A1</marks>
- Practice regularly in a quiet environment to improve concentration <marks>A1</marks>
- Combine imagery with breathing or relaxation techniques to manage arousal <marks>A1</marks>
- Focus on positive and successful outcomes to boost self-efficacy <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

questions.specification: Why is goal setting considered effective for exercise adherence?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why goal setting is effective for exercise adherence, emphasizing providing direction and maintaining motivation—core to goal setting as a psychological skill to enhance motivation via outcome/performance goals.

options.content: It only benefits elite athletes who already have high motivation
It reduces the need to monitor progress because the goal provides the outcome
It makes exercise adherence automatic by removing the need for self-discipline
It provides direction and maintains motivation over time
